If possible, verify the text with references provided in the foreign-language article. * You **must** provide [copyright attribution](/wiki/Wikipedia:Copying_within_Wikipedia "Wikipedia:Copying within Wikipedia") in the [edit summary](/wiki/Help:Edit_summary "Help:Edit summary") accompanying your translation by providing an [interlanguage link](/wiki/Help:Interlanguage_links "Help:Interlanguage links") to the source of your translation. A model attribution edit summary is `Content in this edit is translated from the existing Dutch Wikipedia article at [[:nl:Unie van Utrecht (1579)]]; see its history for attribution.` * You may also add the template `{{Translated|nl|Unie van Utrecht (1579)}}` to the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Union_of_Utrecht "Talk:Union of Utrecht"). * For more guidance, see [Wikipedia:Translation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Translation "Wikipedia:Translation"). |

([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Unie van Utrecht*) was an alliance based on an agreement concluded on 23 January 1579 between a number of [Dutch provinces](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands") and cities, to reach a joint commitment against the king, [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"). By joining forces, they hoped to force him to stop his harsh administrative measures. In addition, some important political matters were regulated in areas such as defence, taxation and religion, which is why the treaty in question is also seen as a first version or precursor of a later constitution. The Union of Utrecht complemented the so-called [General Union of 1576](/w/index.php?title=General_Union_of_1576&action=edit&redlink=1 "General Union of 1576 (page does not exist)"), established by the [Pacification of Ghent](/wiki/Pacification_of_Ghent "Pacification of Ghent"), which is why it is also referred to as the Further Union.

outgoing_links.target_title: Sovereign Principality of the United Netherlands
States of Holland and West Friesland
Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba

1579 treaty unifying the northern Netherlands provinces

For the federation of Old Catholic churches, see [Union of Utrecht (Old Catholic)](/wiki/Union_of_Utrecht_(Old_Catholic) "Union of Utrecht (Old Catholic)").

The **Union of Utrecht** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Unie van Utrecht*) was an alliance based on an agreement concluded on 23 January 1579 between a number of [Dutch provinces](/wiki/Habsburg_Netherlands "Habsburg Netherlands") and cities, to reach a joint commitment against the king, [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"). By joining forces, they hoped to force him to stop his harsh administrative measures. In addition, some important political matters were regulated in areas such as defence, taxation and religion, which is why the treaty in question is also seen as a first version or precursor of a later constitution. The Union of Utrecht complemented the so-called [General Union of 1576](/w/index.php?title=General_Union_of_1576&action=edit&redlink=1 "General Union of 1576 (page does not exist)"), established by the [Pacification of Ghent](/wiki/Pacification_of_Ghent "Pacification of Ghent"), which is why it is also referred to as the Further Union.

## Previous history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_of_Utrecht&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Previous history")]

### Earlier alliances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_of_Utrecht&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Earlier alliances")]

The signing of the treaty for the Union of Utrecht, during the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War") (1568–1648), was preceded by a whole series of unions, edicts and covenants. At the [Union of Dordrecht](/w/index.php?title=Union_of_Dordrecht&action=edit&redlink=1 "Union of Dordrecht (page does not exist)"), on 4 July 1575, [William of Orange](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") was appointed [stadholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") of Holland and Holland and Zeeland decided to cooperate. These areas – except for Amsterdam and Middelburg, among others – were largely free of Spanish troops in the years 1572–1576, and there leaders with the [Calvinist](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") faith gained the upper hand. The [Spanish sack of Antwerp](/wiki/Sack_of_Antwerp "Sack of Antwerp") on 4 November 1576, in which Spanish troops looted and reduced the city to ashes and killed thousands of citizens, caused a stir in the Netherlands. The [States of Holland](/wiki/States_of_Holland_and_West_Friesland "States of Holland and West Friesland"), the States of Zeeland and the other regions, which were predominantly [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), reconciled on 8 November with the Pacification of Ghent in their aversion to the Spanish presence. They declared at the Pacification that they would cooperate in resisting interventions by King [Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") but remain obedient to him. There was no final settlement of the religious issue. For the time being, the Calvinist religion would be leading in Holland and Zeeland, the Catholic religion in the other regions, but religious peace would be sought in all regions.

In Holland and Zeeland, however, Calvinists took little notice of the agreements. And returning Calvinist exiles who had once fled from [Alva](/wiki/Fernando_%C3%81lvarez_de_Toledo,_3rd_Duke_of_Alba "Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba") sometimes caused serious religious disturbances in the then church- and king-faithful regions outside Holland and Zeeland after 1576.

The agreements of the Pacification of Ghent were confirmed at the first [Union of Brussels](/wiki/Union_of_Brussels "Union of Brussels") on 6 January 1577, by which the regions wanted to force the new governor [Don Juan of Austria](/wiki/John_of_Austria "John of Austria") to recognise the Pacification; the Spanish soldiers were to leave the country and the regions themselves would take care of maintaining Catholicism, much against the wishes of the Calvinists. The governor finally agreed by signing the [Eternal Edict](/wiki/Edict_of_1577 "Edict of 1577") on 12 February, after which Spanish troops began to withdraw, largely to the [Duchy of Luxembourg](/wiki/Duchy_of_Luxembourg "Duchy of Luxembourg"), which had always remained royalist. That same month, William of Orange urged Guelders to "form a good, firm alliance and bond in private with those of Holland and Zeeland, as well as other provinces with some of the leading lords and nobles." Rather than break the Pacification, the prince wanted to raise a second line of defence in a ‘further union’. The concept of the Union of Utrecht was already contained in this proposal to Gelre. However, the predominantly Catholic Gelre saw little point in it; it did not want to depend on intransigent, Calvinist Holland for a possible reconciliation with the king.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_of_Utrecht&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

The Union of Utrecht is regarded as the foundation of the [Republic of the Seven United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), which was not recognized by the Spanish Empire until the [Twelve Years' Truce](/wiki/Twelve_Years%27_Truce "Twelve Years' Truce") in 1609.

The treaty was signed on 23 January by [Holland](/wiki/County_of_Holland "County of Holland"), [Zeeland](/wiki/County_of_Zeeland "County of Zeeland"), [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht_(province) "Utrecht (province)") (but not all of Utrecht), and the province (but not the city) of [Groningen](/wiki/Ommelanden "Ommelanden"). The treaty was a reaction of the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") provinces to the 1579 [Union of Arras](/wiki/Union_of_Arras "Union of Arras") (Dutch: *Unie van Atrecht*), in which two southern provinces and a city declared their support for [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") Spain.

During the following months of 1579, other states signed the treaty as well, such as [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), cities from [Friesland](/wiki/Friesland "Friesland"), as well as three of the quarters of [Guelders](/wiki/Guelders "Guelders") ([Nijmegen Quarter](/wiki/Nijmegen_Quarter "Nijmegen Quarter"), [Veluwe Quarter](/wiki/Veluwe_Quarter "Veluwe Quarter"), [Zutphen County](/wiki/Zutphen_County "Zutphen County")). In the summer of 1579, [Amersfoort](/wiki/Amersfoort "Amersfoort") from the province of Utrecht also joined, together with [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres"), [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Breda](/wiki/Breda_(Netherlands) "Breda (Netherlands)") and [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). In February 1580, [Lier](/wiki/Lier,_Belgium "Lier, Belgium"), [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and the surrounding area also signed the treaty. The city of [Groningen](/wiki/Groningen_(city) "Groningen (city)") shifted in favor under influence of the [stadtholder](/wiki/Stadtholder "Stadtholder") for Friesland, [George van Rennenberg](/wiki/George_van_Rennenberg "George van Rennenberg"), and also signed the treaty. The fourth quarter of Guelders, [Upper Guelders](/wiki/Upper_Guelders "Upper Guelders"), never signed the treaty. In April 1580, [Overijssel](/wiki/Overijssel "Overijssel") and [Drenthe](/wiki/Drenthe "Drenthe") signed on.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Map_Union_of_Arras_and_Utrecht_1579-en.svg/250px-Map_Union_of_Arras_and_Utrecht_1579-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_Union_of_Arras_and_Utrecht_1579-en.svg)

Map of the Spanish Netherlands, the Union of Utrecht and the Union of Arras (1579)

The parts of the Low Countries that joined:[[1]](#cite_note-1)

* the [County of Holland](/wiki/County_of_Holland "County of Holland")
* the [County of Zeeland](/wiki/County_of_Zeeland "County of Zeeland")
* the [Lordship of Utrecht](/wiki/Lordship_of_Utrecht "Lordship of Utrecht")
* the [Duchy of Guelders](/wiki/Duchy_of_Guelders "Duchy of Guelders")
* the [Lordship of Groningen](/wiki/Lordship_of_Groningen "Lordship of Groningen")
* the [Lordship of Friesland](/wiki/Lordship_of_Friesland "Lordship of Friesland")
* the [County of Drenthe](/wiki/County_of_Drenthe "County of Drenthe")
* the [Lordship of Overijssel](/wiki/Lordship_of_Overijssel "Lordship of Overijssel")
* the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant")
* the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders")
* the cities of [Tournai](/wiki/Tournai "Tournai") and [Valenciennes](/wiki/Valenciennes "Valenciennes")

Antwerp was the capital of the union until [its fall to the Spanish](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Flanders was almost entirely conquered by the Spanish troops, as was half of Brabant. The United Provinces still recognized Spanish rule after the Union of Utrecht. However, the union contributed to the deterioration in the relationship between the provinces and their lord, and in 1581 the United Provinces declared their independence of the king in the [Act of Abjuration](/wiki/Act_of_Abjuration "Act of Abjuration").

The Twelve Years' Truce of 1609 marked a pause in what became known as the [Eighty Years' War](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"), effectively acknowledging Dutch independence. As Pieter Geyl puts it, the truce marked "an astonishing victory for the Dutch," who surrendered no lands and did not agree to halt their attacks on Spanish colonies and the Spanish trade empire. In return the Spanish granted the United Provinces *de facto* independence by describing them as "Free lands, provinces and states against whom they make no claim" for the duration of the truce.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Religious tolerance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_of_Utrecht&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Religious tolerance")]

The Union of Utrecht allowed complete personal [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") and was thus one of the first unlimited [edicts of religious toleration](/wiki/Edict_of_toleration "Edict of toleration").[[4]](#cite_note-4) An additional declaration allowed provinces and cities that wished to remain Roman Catholic to join the union.

## Later reflection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Union_of_Utrecht&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Later reflection")]

Many people view the Union of Utrecht as the beginning of the Netherlands as a single state. This is not entirely accurate. It can be said that the Union of Utrecht laid the foundation for the Dutch Republic, also known as the Seven United Provinces, which would be formed a few years later. However, these seven states within a state only became a centralised state during the time of the [Batavian Republic](/wiki/Batavian_Republic "Batavian Republic") two centuries later.

Until the early 20th century, most Dutch and Belgian historians, such as P. L. Muller (1867) and [Henri Pirenne](/wiki/Henri_Pirenne "Henri Pirenne") (1911), believed that the Union of Utrecht was initially intended as a "Calvinist alliance" of the "seven" "Northern provinces" that separated themselves from the General Union (the Pacification of Ghent and the Unions of Brussels), and "seceded from the South".[[5]](#cite_note-5): 454 The few Southern cities that joined the Union of Utrecht were considered more like "honorary members" rather than fully integrated members of the "Northern" Union.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Durme1944454-6) Flemish researcher Leo Delfos further investigated and openly challenged this view from 1929 onwards.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Durme1944454-6) He concluded that the Union of Utrecht actually sought to uphold the General Union / Pacification of Ghent of 1576 and did not intend to geographically limit itself to the North, but aimed to include all provinces in the Netherlands.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Durme1944455-7) Both the Pacification and the Union of Utrecht were, in fact, treaties between two parties: the Calvinist-governed provinces of Holland and Zeeland and the other 'fifteen' provinces dominated by Catholics.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Durme1944455-7) Even Alexander Farnese (Parma), the archenemy of the Union of Utrecht, denied in a letter to the States of Artois dated January 27, 1579, that the newly established Union of Utrecht had a Calvinist foundation. It was only through Parma's military conquests in the 1580s and the political developments in the rebellious region that it gradually became, in practice, a 'Northern Calvinist alliance', but it certainly did not start that way.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Durme1944455-7)
